title: OCCT dedicated kernel thread
analysis:
OcctKernel (lib.rs:22-31) has an unsound `unsafe impl Send + Sync` that works only because nothing currently shares the kernel across threads. OCCT uses process-global state (memory allocators, shape naming tables, Standard_Failure exception state, STEP writer state) making concurrent access UB. M4's concurrent evaluation will spawn multiple async tasks that could access the kernel simultaneously.

The fix is a dedicated-thread actor pattern. OcctKernelHandle holds a tokio::sync::mpsc::Sender<OcctRequest> to communicate with a std::thread that owns the real OcctKernel. Each GeometryKernel method sends a request with a oneshot reply channel and awaits the response via blocking_recv. OcctKernelHandle is naturally Send+Sync (Sender<T> is Send+Sync when T: Send).

Key complications: (1) export() takes `&mut dyn Write` which is !Send — the kernel thread exports to Vec<u8>, sends bytes back, handle writes to caller's writer. (2) After removing unsafe impls, OcctKernel can't satisfy GeometryKernel's Send+Sync supertraits (cxx::UniquePtr<OcctShape> is !Send), so method bodies become inherent methods. (3) Three external call sites (cli/main.rs:134, e2e_bracket.rs:42, boundary4_geometry.rs occt_tests with 9 instances) must migrate to OcctKernelHandle.

The implementation uses std::thread::spawn (not tokio::spawn) because OCCT operations are blocking CPU-bound FFI calls that would starve the tokio runtime. Channel shutdown is signaled by dropping the Sender (idiomatic Rust — no explicit Shutdown variant needed). All existing OcctKernel method bodies are preserved verbatim as inherent methods.

All types crossing the channel (GeometryOp, GeometryQuery, GeometryHandle, Value, Mesh, all error types) are already Send. GeometryOp and GeometryQuery are Clone (needed for channel send). tokio is already a workspace dep with the 'sync' feature providing mpsc and oneshot channels.

--- POST-REVIEW ADDITIONS ---

Review identified two blocking robustness issues:

(1) async_context_panic: All four dispatch methods (execute, query, export, tessellate) use tokio::sync::mpsc::Sender::blocking_send and tokio::sync::oneshot::Receiver::blocking_recv, both of which panic unconditionally when called from within an async execution context. M4's concurrent evaluation will spawn async tasks via ConcurrentScheduler (reify-runtime/src/concurrent.rs) that use tokio::spawn — any call to the sync GeometryKernel methods from these tasks panics at runtime with no compile-time warning.

Fix: Add async companion methods (execute_async, query_async, export_async, tessellate_async) that use tx.send().await + reply_rx.await. These are safe to call from any async context. The sync methods remain for non-async callers but get prominent # Panics documentation. The async methods are inherent on OcctKernelHandle (not on the GeometryKernel trait, which is sync-only). export_async returns Result<Vec<u8>, ExportError> instead of taking &mut dyn Write, because writer references cannot be held across await points and would make the future !Send.

(2) blocking_in_async_drop: Drop::drop calls thread.join(), which blocks the calling thread. If OcctKernelHandle is dropped inside an async task (guaranteed in M4 during cancellation or engine teardown), this blocks a tokio worker thread for the duration of whatever OCCT operation is in flight — a well-known deadlock/starvation vector.

Fix: In Drop, check tokio::runtime::Handle::try_current(). If inside async context, drop the sender (closing the channel so the kernel thread exits naturally) but do NOT join — detach the thread instead. If outside async context, preserve existing behavior (drop sender, then join). Additionally, provide pub async fn shutdown(self) that drops the sender then uses tokio::task::spawn_blocking to join the thread, giving async callers an explicit clean-shutdown path. Requires adding 'rt' feature to tokio dep for Handle::try_current().

prerequisites.description: Add tokio = { workspace = true } dependency to crates/reify-kernel-occt/Cargo.toml. Tokio is already a workspace dep with 'sync' feature (Cargo.toml:43) which provides mpsc and oneshot channels.

Ensure tokio dependency in crates/reify-kernel-occt/Cargo.toml includes the 'rt' feature (needed for tokio::runtime::Handle::try_current() in the async-safe Drop). Also ensure 'macros' and 'rt-multi-thread' are available as dev-dependencies for #[tokio::test]. If the workspace dep already includes these features, no change needed; otherwise add features = ["rt"] to the dependency specification.

steps.description: Write compile-time Send+Sync assertion test for OcctKernelHandle (similar to DispatchPlanner pattern in reify-geometry/src/lib.rs:20-23). Write unit test that OcctKernelHandle::spawn() returns a handle and does not panic. Test in a new module `handle` within lib.rs or a new file handle.rs.

Create OcctRequest enum with variants: Execute{op: GeometryOp, reply: oneshot::Sender<Result<GeometryHandle, GeometryError>>}, Query{query: GeometryQuery, reply: oneshot::Sender<Result<Value, QueryError>>}, Export{handle: GeometryHandleId, format: ExportFormat, reply: oneshot::Sender<Result<Vec<u8>, ExportError>>}, Tessellate{handle: GeometryHandleId, tolerance: f64, reply: oneshot::Sender<Result<Mesh, TessError>>}. Create OcctKernelHandle struct with tx: tokio::sync::mpsc::Sender<OcctRequest> and thread: Option<std::thread::JoinHandle<()>>. Implement spawn() that creates mpsc channel, spawns std::thread with recv loop, returns handle.
Write test: handle.execute() creates a box primitive and returns a valid GeometryHandle with id=GeometryHandleId(1) and repr=Solid. Uses blocking channel send/recv.

Implement execute() on OcctKernelHandle: creates oneshot channel, sends Execute request via tx.blocking_send(), receives result via reply_rx.blocking_recv(). Handle channel errors by converting to GeometryError::OperationFailed. Implement Execute handling in kernel thread loop: call self.kernel.execute(op), send result on reply channel.
Write test: handle.query() — create box, query Volume, verify result is Value::Real with correct volume. Also test query with invalid handle returns QueryError::InvalidHandle.
Implement query() on OcctKernelHandle: sends Query request, blocks on reply. Implement Query handling in kernel thread loop.
Write test: handle.export() — create box, export to STEP, verify output contains ISO-10303-21 header. Also test export with unsupported format returns ExportError.

Implement export() on OcctKernelHandle: sends Export request (with handle id and format, no writer). Kernel thread calls OcctKernel::export() with a Vec<u8> as writer, sends Vec<u8> back. Handle receives bytes and writes them to caller's &mut dyn Write. Implement Export handling in kernel thread loop.
Write test: handle.tessellate() — create box, tessellate with tolerance 0.1, verify mesh has vertices (len > 0), indices (len > 0, divisible by 3), and normals.
Implement tessellate() on OcctKernelHandle: sends Tessellate request, blocks on reply. Implement Tessellate handling in kernel thread loop.

Write test: OcctKernelHandle implements GeometryKernel — box it as Box<dyn GeometryKernel>, execute an op through trait interface, verify success. Compile-time const assertion that OcctKernelHandle: GeometryKernel + Send + Sync.

Implement `impl GeometryKernel for OcctKernelHandle`. Each trait method delegates to the channel-based inherent methods. execute(&mut self) calls self.execute(), query(&self) calls self.query(), etc. The handle methods only need &self (channel send is &self on Sender) so &mut self for execute works trivially.

Write test: error propagation — Chamfer (unimplemented) returns GeometryError::OperationFailed through channel. Export with invalid handle returns ExportError::InvalidHandle. Tessellate with invalid handle returns TessError::InvalidHandle.

Ensure all error variants propagate correctly through channels. Add kernel-thread-died error handling: if oneshot recv fails (thread panicked), return appropriate error type with message 'kernel thread died'.

Write test: drop OcctKernelHandle, verify thread exits cleanly. Create handle, execute an op (verify kernel thread is alive), drop handle, verify no panic/leak. Test that creating multiple handles sequentially works (each gets its own thread).

Implement Drop for OcctKernelHandle: drop the Sender (causes kernel thread recv to return Err/None), then join the thread via self.thread.take().map(|t| t.join()). Handle join errors gracefully (thread may have panicked).

Write test: multi-operation sequence through handle — create box, create cylinder, boolean union, fillet, query volume, tessellate, export STEP. Verifies stateful kernel operations work correctly across channel boundary with handle reuse.

Remove `unsafe impl Send for OcctKernel` and `unsafe impl Sync for OcctKernel` (lines 30-31 of lib.rs). Convert `impl GeometryKernel for OcctKernel` block to inherent methods: pub fn execute(&mut self, op: &GeometryOp) -> Result<GeometryHandle, GeometryError>, pub fn query(&self, ...), pub fn export(&self, ...), pub fn tessellate(&self, ...). Bodies stay identical. OcctKernel is now !Send+!Sync naturally (cxx::UniquePtr is !Send).

Update boundary4_geometry.rs occt_tests (lines 130-380+): replace `use reify_kernel_occt::OcctKernel` with `use reify_kernel_occt::OcctKernelHandle` and `OcctKernel::new()` with `OcctKernelHandle::spawn()`. Remove `use reify_types::GeometryKernel` import since handle methods can be called directly or through the trait. Update e2e_bracket.rs:42 similarly. Update cli/main.rs:134 to use OcctKernelHandle::spawn(). Verify all tests pass.

Final cleanup: add module-level documentation to handle.rs explaining the dedicated-thread pattern and why it's needed. Export OcctKernelHandle prominently from lib.rs. Add doc comment to OcctKernel noting it is !Send+!Sync and should be used via OcctKernelHandle for cross-thread scenarios. Run full test suite to verify no regressions.

[async_context_panic fix] Write #[tokio::test] tests for all four async companion methods on OcctKernelHandle. (1) execute_async: create a box, verify GeometryHandle has id=GeometryHandleId(1) and repr=Solid — confirms execute works from within async context without panic. (2) query_async: create box, query Volume, verify Value::Real with correct volume (~6000); also test query on invalid handle returns QueryError::InvalidHandle. (3) export_async: create box, call export_async (returns Result<Vec<u8>, ExportError>), verify returned bytes contain ISO-10303-21 header; test export of invalid handle returns ExportError::InvalidHandle. (4) tessellate_async: create box, tessellate, verify mesh has vertices (non-empty), indices (non-empty, divisible by 3), and normals. (5) async_multi_op_sequence: full pipeline via async methods — create box, cylinder, union, fillet via execute_async, query volume via query_async, tessellate via tessellate_async, export via export_async. All tests run inside #[tokio::test] proving the async methods are safe in async context.

[async_context_panic fix] Implement four async companion methods on OcctKernelHandle. (1) pub async fn execute_async(&self, op: &GeometryOp) -> Result<GeometryHandle, GeometryError>: clones op, creates oneshot, uses self.tx.send().await + reply_rx.await. (2) pub async fn query_async(&self, query: &GeometryQuery) -> Result<Value, QueryError>: clones query, same pattern. (3) pub async fn export_async(&self, handle: GeometryHandleId, format: ExportFormat) -> Result<Vec<u8>, ExportError>: returns Vec<u8> directly (no &mut dyn Write — writer references cannot be held across await points and would make the future !Send). (4) pub async fn tessellate_async(&self, handle: GeometryHandleId, tolerance: f64) -> Result<Mesh, TessError>: handle and tolerance are Copy, straightforward. All channel errors map to domain errors with 'kernel thread died' message, matching the sync methods. Add # Panics doc section on each of the four sync methods (execute, query, export, tessellate) stating: 'Panics if called from within a tokio async execution context. Use the _async variant instead.' Addresses reviewer issue async_context_panic.

[blocking_in_async_drop fix] Write #[tokio::test] test that creates OcctKernelHandle, runs an async op (execute_async), then drops the handle inside the async context — must complete without blocking the tokio worker thread or panicking. Write #[tokio::test] test for pub async fn shutdown(self): create handle, execute_async an op, call handle.shutdown().await, verify it completes cleanly and the kernel thread has exited (subsequent spawn creates a fresh kernel). Write #[test] (non-async) verifying the sync Drop path still joins the thread (existing behavior preserved): create handle, execute op, drop, no panic.

[blocking_in_async_drop fix] Modify Drop::drop to be async-context-safe. Use tokio::runtime::Handle::try_current() to detect if running inside an async executor. If inside async context: drop the sender (replace with dummy to close channel, signaling kernel thread to exit via recv returning None) but do NOT call thread.join() — the thread is detached and will exit naturally when its recv loop sees the closed channel. If outside async context: preserve existing behavior (drop sender, then join thread for deterministic cleanup). Add pub async fn shutdown(self) method: explicitly drops the sender, then calls tokio::task::spawn_blocking(move || thread.join()) to await thread exit without blocking the async worker. This gives async callers an explicit clean-shutdown path when they need deterministic resource cleanup. Document the async drop behavior in the struct-level doc comment. Addresses reviewer issue blocking_in_async_drop.

design_decisions.decision: Use tokio::sync::mpsc + tokio::sync::oneshot for channels with blocking_recv()/blocking_send() on the sync side
design_decisions.rationale: GeometryKernel trait methods are synchronous (not async). The handle must block waiting for the kernel thread's reply. tokio::sync channels are already available (workspace dep) and their blocking_recv() method works outside async context. This avoids adding std::sync::mpsc as a separate dependency. The kernel thread uses blocking recv on the mpsc receiver naturally.

design_decisions.decision: Use std::thread::spawn for the kernel thread, not tokio::spawn
design_decisions.rationale: OCCT operations are CPU-bound blocking FFI calls. Running them on a tokio task would block the async runtime's thread pool. A dedicated OS thread is correct — it has its own stack and never contends with async work. The thread runs a simple synchronous loop: recv request → execute on kernel → send reply.

design_decisions.decision: Export serialization happens on the kernel thread, raw bytes returned via channel
design_decisions.rationale: GeometryKernel::export() takes `&mut dyn Write` which is !Send (trait objects with non-Send are !Send). We cannot send the writer to the kernel thread. Instead, the kernel thread writes to a Vec<u8> on the kernel thread, sends the Vec<u8> back through the channel, and the handle writes the bytes to the caller's writer. This adds one memory copy but maintains complete thread safety.

design_decisions.decision: Convert OcctKernel's GeometryKernel trait impl to inherent methods
design_decisions.rationale: GeometryKernel trait has `Send + Sync` supertraits. Once we remove `unsafe impl Send + Sync`, OcctKernel can't implement GeometryKernel (cxx::UniquePtr<OcctShape> is !Send). Rather than modifying the trait definition in reify-types (which would affect all consumers), we move the method bodies to inherent methods with identical signatures. The kernel thread calls these directly. Only OcctKernelHandle implements GeometryKernel.

design_decisions.decision: OcctKernelHandle stores Option<JoinHandle> and joins on Drop
design_decisions.rationale: Clean shutdown: dropping the handle drops the mpsc Sender, the kernel thread's recv loop sees the channel closed (recv returns None) and exits cleanly, then Drop joins the thread. This ensures OCCT resources (shapes, allocators) are properly freed. The Option wrapper allows take() in Drop.

design_decisions.decision: Channel errors (kernel thread panic) map to domain-specific error types
design_decisions.rationale: If the kernel thread panics (e.g., OCCT triggers an unhandled C++ exception), the oneshot receiver gets a RecvError. We convert this to GeometryError::OperationFailed('kernel thread died'), QueryError::QueryFailed('kernel thread died'), etc. rather than panicking the caller. This keeps error handling composable.

design_decisions.decision: Keep OcctKernel pub but document it as !Send+!Sync
design_decisions.rationale: Advanced single-threaded test code may want direct access without channel overhead. OcctKernel remains pub with inherent methods but documented as !Send+!Sync and not implementing GeometryKernel. OcctKernelHandle is the recommended API for all cross-thread usage.

design_decisions.decision: Migrate external call sites (boundary4, e2e_bracket, CLI) to OcctKernelHandle
design_decisions.rationale: Since OcctKernel no longer implements GeometryKernel (missing Send+Sync bounds), it can't be passed as Box<dyn GeometryKernel> to Engine. All external sites that create OcctKernel for use with Engine must switch to OcctKernelHandle::spawn(). This is a mechanical change: replace `OcctKernel::new()` with `OcctKernelHandle::spawn()`.

design_decisions.decision: Async companion methods are inherent on OcctKernelHandle, not on the GeometryKernel trait
design_decisions.rationale: The GeometryKernel trait is synchronous (fn, not async fn) and used across the codebase. Adding async methods would require modifying the trait in reify-types, affecting all consumers. Instead, the async methods (execute_async, query_async, export_async, tessellate_async) are inherent methods on OcctKernelHandle. M4's async tasks will call these directly on OcctKernelHandle (via Arc or reference) rather than through Box<dyn GeometryKernel>. A future AsyncGeometryKernel trait could be added if needed.

design_decisions.decision: export_async returns Result<Vec<u8>, ExportError> instead of taking &mut dyn Write
design_decisions.rationale: In async context, &mut dyn Write cannot be held across await points (it is not necessarily Send, making the future !Send and incompatible with tokio::spawn). The sync export already gets Vec<u8> from the kernel thread internally then writes to the caller's writer. The async version simply returns the Vec<u8> directly — callers write the bytes themselves after awaiting. This keeps the future Send and is a cleaner API for async callers.

design_decisions.decision: Drop detects async context via Handle::try_current() and detaches thread instead of joining
design_decisions.rationale: thread.join() in Drop blocks the calling thread. If dropped inside an async task (M4 cancellation, engine teardown), this blocks a tokio worker thread — a deadlock/starvation vector. When Handle::try_current() succeeds (async context), Drop drops the sender (closing the channel so the kernel thread exits naturally via recv returning None) but detaches the thread instead of joining. The thread is guaranteed to exit because its recv loop will see the closed channel. OCCT resources are still freed — just asynchronously when the thread exits. For callers needing deterministic cleanup from async code, shutdown() is provided.

reuse.where: Workspace dependency (Cargo.toml:43), already used in crates/reify-runtime for concurrent scheduling
reuse.how: Add `tokio = { workspace = true }` to reify-kernel-occt/Cargo.toml. Use mpsc::channel(32) for request stream, oneshot::channel() for per-request replies. Use blocking_send/blocking_recv for sync GeometryKernel trait methods. Use send().await/recv().await for async companion methods.

reuse.what: GeometryKernel trait definition and all error types
reuse.how: OcctKernelHandle implements the existing GeometryKernel trait unchanged. All error types (GeometryError, QueryError, ExportError, TessError) are Clone and Send, flowing through channels as-is.
Method bodies stay identical — only change is moving from `impl GeometryKernel for OcctKernel` to inherent `impl OcctKernel`. The kernel thread calls these directly.
Copy the `const _: fn() = || { fn must_be_send_sync<T: Send + Sync>() {} must_be_send_sync::<OcctKernelHandle>(); };` pattern for OcctKernelHandle.

reuse.what: Test patterns from boundary4_geometry.rs and reify-geometry tests
reuse.how: Follow same structure: create kernel handle, execute ops, verify handles/results, test error paths. The OcctKernelHandle tests mirror these patterns through the channel layer.

reuse.where: tokio crate ('rt' feature), standard pattern documented in tokio docs
reuse.how: In Drop::drop, call Handle::try_current(). Ok(_) means we're inside an async executor — must not block. Err(_) means sync context — safe to join. This is the idiomatic tokio pattern for runtime-aware Drop implementations.
